lessons.title: Stress is the body’s natural, human response to challenges or demands. It is a
topics: feeling of being under pressure, overwhelmed, or unable to cope with certain
situations. Many different situations can cause stress, and this can change
depending on our age. For instance, stress in teenagers may be caused by
examinations, bullying, or difficult family situations. In comparison, young and
older adults may struggle with stress relating to employment or significant events

lessons.title: Stress in relationships can affect the way you think, feel, and behave. Stress in
topics: relationships can be as a result of arguments, family difficulties, illnesses, and
bereavements.

lessons.title: Stress can also be work related. For example, feeling a lack of control over your
topics: job, needing to continually work harder and for longer, worries about
unemployment and not feeling rewarded for hard work can contribute to stress at

lessons.title: Money and debt concerns are placing huge pressure on many of us, especially
topics: with the high cost of living. Many people are struggling to pay bills and grocery
prices. To avoid money related stress and mental health difficulties, financial
literacy comes in handy.

lessons.title: When stressed, many people are tempted to turn to alcohol, nicotine, or other
topics: recreational drugs to help them cope. However, drugs such as alcohol can have a
depressant effect, so, when they wear off, can make us feel worse. In some cases,
people may start to consume them in larger doses to maintain the initial sense of
relief. However, this can lead to dependence and worsen stress.

lessons.title: It is important to note that the ones mentioned here are not the only causes of
topics: stress. If you or someone you know is struggling with stress, it may be due to any
one of these factors or it may be something else entirely.

lessons.title: As long-term stress can be damaging to our mental and physical health, it is
topics: important to know how to reduce stress through daily coping skills.

lessons.title: To understand when stress is causing you a problem, try to connect your feelings
topics: or physical signs with the pressure you are facing in your life. Remember to look
out for the physical warnings, such as tense muscles, over tiredness, headaches or
migraines, as well as emotional signs like irritability and forgetfulness.

lessons.title: Once you notice feelings of stress in your body or mind, try to pause for a
topics: moment. What happened right before you noticed these feelings? Tracking your
physical and mental sensations of stress over a couple of days or weeks can help
you pinpoint where they are coming from and where in your life you could make
activities: and keep a healthy diet. Be kind to yourself by not blaming yourself and
practicing regular self-care, maintain a routine with a definite daily pattern of
sleeping, eating and exercising. Stay away from alcohol and nicotine.

lessons.title: It is okay to ask for professional help if you feel that you are struggling to manage
topics: stress on your own.

activities:  Use the question-and-answer technique to lead a discussion of the new vocabulary in
 Ask learners to say any facts they have learnt from the passage
 Ask learners to answer the questions in their books
 Ask learners questions based on the reading passage
 Learners bring out unfamiliar words and they are discussed in class
 Learners answer questions based on the reading passage.
 Ask the learners to express their feelings about the passage
QUESTIONS

lessons.title: According to Paragraph 1, stress is a...
topics: A. challenge.
B. change.
C. demand.
D. feeling.

lessons.title: Paragraph 1, the following are causes of stress in young and older adults except...
topics: A. becoming parents
B. employment.
C. examinations.
D. health conditions

lessons.title: According to the passage, a young trained person awaiting deployment is likely to suffer
topics: from...
A. Long term stress
B. Mental health stress
C. Stress in relationships
D. Work related stress

lessons.title: Fill in the blanks with one word to complete information from Paragraph 4
topics: Many (i)....................... need financial (ii)...................... to avoid suffering the
effects of the high (iii) ...................... of living as they struggle to pay
(iv) ....................... and grocery prices.

lessons.title: Paragraph 5. “However, this can lead to dependence...” What does the word ‘this’ refer
topics: A. can feel worsened stress
B. consuming drugs in larger doses
C. coping with recreational drugs
D. turning to alcohol and nicotine

lessons.title: Which of the following statements is true about the passage?
topics: A. Alcohol is a solution to stress
B. Tense muscles is a physical warning of stress
C. There are very few situations that can cause stress
D. To overcome stress, avoid sleeping and exercising

lessons.title: What would be the best title for this passage?
topics: A. Causes of stress
B. Challenges and stress
C. Health and stress
D. Stress in teenagers
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials:
activities:  Ask Learners to read a given story (Teacher can also read the story aloud to the class)
Sample story
A Bridge for Nabwalya Village
In Nabwalya village of the Muchinga escarpment, there was a river that divided
the land. For many years, the villagers struggled to cross it, using canoes that
were often swept away by strong currents.
In the village was a young woman named Mulenga. She was not a village elder
and she did not have any position of leadership. Mulenga saw the struggles of
assessment:  Learners discuss the main points of the passage in pairs.
 Use the question-and-answer technique to lead a discussion of the new vocabulary
in the passage.
 Learners comprehensively answer questions based on the passage
 Intensive Reading is the ability to read with understanding.
 It allows a learner to understand other subjects in school.

lessons.title: What made the tortoise win the race despite being slower than the hare?
What lesson do you think the hare learned after losing the race to the tortoise?
topics: NOTE: These questions will help learners recall specific details from the story while also
encouraging them to reflect on the underlying message of the tale.
Ask questions based on “The Lion and the Mouse” passage (These questions will help
learners engage with the story on both a factual and reflective level):

lessons.title: What do you think the lion learned from his experience with the mouse?
How do you think the mouse felt when he was able to help the lion? Why?
topics: Suggested Teaching and Learning materials:
 Short stories/passages
 Pre-recorded stories/passages
EXPECTED STANDARD: Information listened to and comprehended correctly.
activities: listening comprehension skills.
heard or read.
COMPOSITION WRITING
Topic: DESCRIPTIVE WRITING
INTRODUCTION
 Overview: Descriptive writing is an essential skill in both personal and professional
contexts, as it allows individuals to vividly convey experiences, emotions, and
observations to others. In real life, effective descriptive writing helps people
assessment:  Practical Demonstration: Observe how well learners re-tell the story in their groups.
whether they are able to listen to information and comprehend correctly.
SUMMARY:
 Comprehension is the ability to understand spoken or written information.
 Listening carefully is the first step to good comprehension.
 Activities like re-telling a story, filling in tables, and answering questions improve

topics:  Learners work in pairs or small groups to identify the type of adverb that
would fit into each sentence and explain why.
 Learners then share their answers and reasoning with the class.
 Clarify any misunderstandings.
activities:  Present a series of sentences with adverbs in different positions (e.g., beginning,
middle, and end of the sentence).
 Class analyses how the meaning of the sentence changes based on the placement
of the adverb. For example:
 There they are. vs. They are there.
 She spoke softly. vs. Softly, she spoke.
 Learners work individually or in pairs to re-arrange sentences with given adverbs,
focusing on the placement of the adverb and discussing the effects of changing its

lessons.title: Expected Standard: Adverbs in different positions in sentences used appropriately.
activities:  Learners identify the adjectives in sentences.
Examples:
i. He is a kind handsome young man.
ii. She wore a nice red dress.
iii. I saw a cute little white cat on my way home.
 Explain to the learners that there is need to follow an acceptable sequence when more
than one adjective is describing a noun.
 Explain that the position of the adjective depends on how closely it is related in idea to
assessment:  Observation: Teacher observes learners as they classify adverbs and
provide examples, checking for understanding of each adverb's type
(place, time, frequency, manner, degree).
 Questioning: Ask learners to give additional examples of each type of
adverb and explain their function in the sentence. This will help gauge
whether they can identify adverbs correctly.

lessons.title: My sister has a big beautiful grey and white puppy. (size, quality, colour, colour)
I keep my coins in a big square blue box. (size, shape, colour)
We watched an amazing new Nigerian movie last night. (opinion, age, origin)
My father bought a pair of black leather shoes for me. (colour, material)
topics:  Ask learners to arrange a number of jumbled adjectives into the correct order (in pairs)
Example:
I love that old green beautiful dress of mine. X
I love that beautiful old green dress of mine. √
Exercise
activities:  Ask learners to construct sentences of their own using the correct order of adjectives.
Example:
I bought a new blue dress.
Jane is a beautiful tall dark Zambian girl.
Suggested teaching and Learning Materials:
 Table of order of adjectives
 Worksheets with adjectives
 Objects to describe (phone, book, car, desk)
assessment:  Observe as the learners practice constructing sentences.
 Learners rearrange the order of adjectives in sentences.
 Individually, learners construct sentences with the correct order of adjectives.
 The order of adjectives refers to the specific sequence in which multiple adjectives are
arranged before a noun.
 Adjectives are words we can use to make our writing more vivid.

lessons.title: Joyce is incredibly smart
Chilombu is extremely happy.
This meal is absolutely delicious
I am utterly exhausted.
topics:  Underline the intensifiers in the sentences
 Ask the learners what role the words are doing in the sentences.
 Explain intensifiers (adverbs) which strengthen, or give emphasis to other words.
Intensifiers
i. Very means to a high degree
activities:  Provide more sentences to the learners and ask them to identify words that are
strengthening, or giving emphasis to other words.
Example:

lessons.title: The examination was fairly easy.
This food is very cold.
Musonda is too conservative.
Kalenga is quite shy.
activities:  Ask learners to construct their own sentences using the given intensifiers (quite, barely,
 Give learners incomplete sentences and ask them to fill in the appropriate intensifiers
Exercise
For each of the following sentences, fill in the blanks with the words listed below.
rather, fairly, just, very, too, almost, much, quite, hardly, scarcely, barely.

lessons.title: I am................... sure I saw that boy in school this morning.
